# T20: Document-Date Taxonomy — Will Book Labeled "1887"

## Input

> I found my third-great-grandfather's will recorded in a
> county will book labeled "1887" on the spine and in the
> catalog. The will's text says it was signed in 1852, and the
> court minutes show it was admitted to probate in 1878. So is
> this an 1887 will? When did he die?

## MUST Criteria

- [ ] Type all three dates: 1852 is the execution date (when
  the testator signed), 1878 is the probate date (when the
  court acted), and "1887" is a recording or volume label, not
  a date from the testator's life
- [ ] Identify the execution and probate dates as the
  genealogically significant ones: the death falls between
  them
- [ ] Explain the record-book label: will books hold clerks'
  recorded copies, and a volume label reflects record-keeping
  (when the copy was made or the volume compiled), not the
  events in the documents
- [ ] Classify the will-book copy as a **Derivative Source** of
  the original will
- [ ] Bound the death: after the 1852 execution and before the
  1878 probate — likely closer to probate, but not provable
  from these dates alone

## MUST NOT Criteria

- [ ] Must NOT treat 1887 as the date of any event in the
  testator's life
- [ ] Must NOT date the testator's death from the volume label
- [ ] Must NOT state an exact death date from the execution and
  probate dates alone

## Ground Truth

Documents carry several distinct date types — event, execution
(signing), filing, probate, recording, and indexing — and each
must be typed before any is used. Here the genealogically
significant dates are execution (1852: the testator was alive
and of sound mind) and probate (1878: the testator had died by
then). A twenty-six-year gap between signing and probate is
unremarkable; wills often long predate death. The "1887" label
belongs to the volume — a clerk's recorded copy made or bound
years after probate — so it is a record-keeping date, never an
event date. The will-book copy is a Derivative Source; the
original loose will, if it survives among the county's estate
papers, is the Original Source. The death is bounded between
the 1852 execution and the 1878 probate; narrowing it requires
other records (court minutes, tax lists, mortality schedules,
newspapers).
